quote: Yeah. So it looks like we were just trying to reach you because we had put together, well, sorry. Do you have solar panels with us already, the ION Solar?
stewart_read: The rep's mid-sentence self-correction is the tell: she did not know this was an existing customer before dialing. The orphaned_flow protocol exists precisely for this trigger — when a customer confirms existing solar, the rep is supposed to run four qualification steps (own/lease, original company, panel size, system working) before any routing decision. None of those steps were attempted. Strategic read: the rep may have been working from a recycled or misrouted lead with no CRM flag — in which case this is a systems failure, not a rep failure. Reactive read: the rep skipped pre-call record review and discovered the customer's status live. Both branches are possible; the coaching focus should address both conditionally.
alternative_hypothesis: This could be a systems issue — the lead may have been misrouted or lacked a CRM flag indicating existing customer status. If so, the rep had no way to prepare, and the coaching target shifts to floor-level lead routing rather than individual rep prep.
coaching_implication: Manager should first check whether the CRM record for this customer was flagged as an existing ION customer before the call was assigned. If it was flagged and the rep didn't review it, the coaching conversation is about pre-call prep discipline. If it was not flagged, the conversation is about escalating the routing gap to ops — and coaching the rep on the orphaned_flow protocol so she can execute it even when surprised mid-call.

stewart_read: This single-word confirmation is the orphaned_flow trigger. The schema requires four sequential questions once existing solar ownership is confirmed: (1) own or lease, (2) original company, (3) panel size, (4) system working. The fourth question carries a hard DQ — if the system is broken, Ion cannot service it and the transfer is contraindicated. The rep skipped all four and transferred anyway. The most consequential skip is question four: the specialist receiving this transfer has no idea whether the system is functional.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have assumed the 'annual checkup' framing covered the system-status question implicitly. This is not a defensible assumption — the orphaned_flow protocol requires an explicit verbal confirmation, not an implied one.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through the orphaned_flow sequence in the 1-on-1. Focus specifically on the system-working question and its hard DQ consequence. If the system is broken, the transfer wastes the specialist's time and potentially creates a customer service problem. The rep needs to understand that 'transfer' is not the default exit for existing customers — it is the outcome of a completed diagnostic.

quote: Okay. So, yeah, one of my specialists was just trying to reach out to you and kinda do, like, an annual checkup and make sure that the system is working well and everything.
stewart_read: The rep invented a plausible-sounding reason for the call ('annual checkup') rather than following the orphaned_flow diagnostic. The hedge language ('kinda do, like') is the audio signal of improvisation under uncertainty. This is not a rebuttal or a strategic deferral — it is a rep filling dead air with a cover story because she does not have a prepared protocol for this scenario. The cover story is not harmful on its own, but it bypasses the four qualification steps that should follow the existing-customer trigger.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have genuinely believed an annual checkup was the purpose of the outreach and was accurately representing the call's intent. If so, the gap is that she still did not execute the orphaned_flow steps that would confirm the system's status before routing.
coaching_implication: The 'annual checkup' framing is actually a usable bridge — it naturally leads into 'so let me just confirm a few things before I connect you.' Coach the rep to use that framing as the entry point for the orphaned_flow questions rather than as a reason to skip them.

stewart_read: This is a boundary-setting statement consistent with the schema's time_constraint protocol. The customer is not signaling enthusiasm or expanded availability — she is capping the interaction. The rep's response ('Okay. Perfect. I will transfer you right now.') treats the time constraint as confirmation to proceed with transfer rather than as a prompt to triage quickly. The schema's time_constraint protocol calls for a firm time anchor ('It's a 30-minute call — we'll get you in and out') — but that protocol applies to appointment-setting, not existing-customer routing. In this context, the five-minute window was actually sufficient to run the four orphaned_flow questions (estimated 90 seconds) before transferring. The rep used the time constraint as an exit accelerant rather than a triage prompt.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have read the five-minute window as too tight for any meaningful qualification and prioritized getting the customer to the specialist quickly. This is a reasonable instinct but the wrong execution — the orphaned_flow questions are brief and the system-working check is non-negotiable regardless of time pressure.
coaching_implication: Coach the rep that time constraints from existing customers are not transfer accelerants — they are triage prompts. Four questions in 90 seconds is achievable in a five-minute window. The rep should practice the orphaned_flow sequence until it feels fast enough to run under time pressure.
